reviews.review_text: Absolutely phenomenal, I've never had such an immersive experience in any other game.

[b] Subnautica is a survival game on an alien ocean planet. It starts calm with exploration and resource gathering but quickly gets more intense the deeper you go. The world is immersive and the amazing music really adds to the atmosphere and tension. It can feel a bit slow at times but the exploration, mystery and overall vibe make it really worth playing. [/b]

[hr][/hr]
[h1]🎚️ Difficulty[/h1]
🔲 My grandmother could play it
🔲 Easy 
🔲 Normal
☑️ Hard (It is hard until you understand it.)
🔲 A challenge to be reckoned

☑️ Leading to more in-depth researches/thoughts

IDK what it is about this game. Can't play it for just an hour. Nope I start playing and suddenly it's tomorrow. I guess what hooks me is how rewarding progression is. I also like it better than SubZero because the story doesn't get in the way, you push forward more out of curiosity than an objective marker telling you were to go. Building stuff is also fun. I like game 10/10.

Gifted to two people this week.

Probably the best single player game experience I have ever enjoyed. And completed multiple times on hardcore, including runs with multiple prohibitions, such as no use of air tanks, rebreather, or bases, except where necessary to unlock subsequent recipes. 

Although, I failed my first 8 attempts at hardcore with no restrictions, cumulatively totalling 14 hours of losses.

You won't regret playing this game. My first playthrough was 78 hours, first accessing the wiki at 74 hours. But I would expect a first playthrough for most people to be around 18-30 hours.

Had to come back and update my review. I may be in the minority here, but after 30 hours of gameplay, I'm just kinda over this game. Was fun and engaging at first, but the constant slog of having to revisit the same areas to farm materials for the next thing I need to build in order to progress the game has lost its charm. The lack of general direction doesn't help either. On several occasions, I found myself having to Google where to go next, only to discover that I had previously been in the right area, but had no idea. Same with tracking down PDAs. It's super frustrating knowing you were just within a few meters of the next PDA that would advance the story, but have no idea if it's there or not. 

Ultimately, the combination of unnecessarily confusing upgrade systems, lack of direction, and constant slog of farming materials really just burned me out on this one. I'm glad I gave it a chance, but it's in the No Man's Sky category for me where it's just not engaging enough to keep me playing.
amazing game, interesting story, fine graphics. few bugs and caveats at exploring and building, but there are plenty of tutorials online and workarounds! definitely recommend

I highly recommend this game to everyone looking to buy it. Even with my underpowered laptop this game still runs very well at low settings and the low settings still look excellent. There is so much to do and explore in this game with so many creatures, biomes, resources, etc. Subnautica never fails to keep you engaged and interested.
I played Subnautica years ago on the ps4 and it still hits the same. The living large update is also a great improvement - can't wait to see how Subnautica 2 will turn out.

Was enjoying the game, then while exploring my vehicle (seamoth) teleported under the ground. It had storage and in that were all the green cubes and purple tablets of the game. I accidentally saved after so I lost 25 hours of progress... This has been a bug for almost a decade that they haven't fixed.

I bought Subnautica mainly to play it in co-op with my girlfriend using the Nitrox mod. Since it’s an unofficial mod, you might run into a few minor desync issues, but I assure you it makes the experience 10 times better. The base game is already a masterpiece in its own right, but building your own base and trying to survive together adds an insane level of fun. Fantastic game, highly recommended, especially if you have a little patience to set up the mod and play it with friends!

Subnautica is probably the best game set underwater. I don’t play survival games very often, but this one hooked me so much that I became addicted to it for several days. I’m always surprised by how an indie studio can create such a complex game that also looks genuinely amazing. On top of that, there’s a great mix of a beautiful underwater world combined with horror elements. The gameplay feels very smooth, the music is incredible, and the story is excellent, even though it’s told in an unusual way, mainly through reading logs and recordings. I also love the Cyclops mechanic, where you can essentially turn it into your own mobile fortress. Overall, this game gave me something I hadn’t experienced in other games before, which is a fear of the deep ocean while also being amazed by everything that can be discovered within it.

Gameplay - 10/10
Story - 10/10
Graphics - 10/10
Pacing - 10/10
Optimalization - 9/10
Soundtrack - 10/10
Audio - 10/10
Pretty cool story survival exploration game. Game gets ya hooked with how well the world and the story connect. Do recommend, ye yee
if you're scared of creatures in deep bodies of water, you will ♥♥♥♥ yourself, but besides that this game is incredible
Still pretty fun after replaying it many times extremely fun with mods 8/10 definitely a must try

Best Subnautica game that will ever be made, especially for this era. Playing as an under prepared silent protagonist isolated on a very ominous patch of submerged land where ancient secrets are buried really helps frame how terrifying the concept is. It has unmatched fear factor, zero hand holding, minimal dialogue, all show no tell, terrifying yet stunning environment and ambience, and when it's dark it's DARK making you more careful what you might run into, great environmental story telling, visuals are easy on the eyes and the soundtrack is so well paced during gameplay. The only game I've ever been too scared to play and too excited not to every time a load a new save.
5 Stars beginner friendly. Very calming and peaceful experience. Go to behind aurora, dunes near sunbeam island and blood kelp zone to enjoy beautiful scenery and get some rare loots!
Fantastic game, absolute must-play for everyone, even non-gamers, to be totally honest.

I'm one of those rare people that manage to avoid knowing anything about Subnautica going into this game blind I wasn't expecting much and it honestly took me a while to get into the game, but as soon as I established a habitat and a home for myself I became obsessed. The base building is just so fun to mess around with I personally just make one giant super base and it was sick, speaking of sick THE CYCLOPS I love that thing so much and honestly made the game so much more fun and 10x better than I thought it could ever be. I'm not the type of person to write long reviews I like to keep it short and simple, but to be transparent I felt guilty because a reason I avoided knowing anything about the game is because I didn't really care for it when it came out and just brushed it off as a boring ocean, I would happily like to say this game proved me wrong and then some.

Played this game all the way through for the first time. What a great game. As a Dad my time is limited so had to look online for guidance but it did not ruin the immersion. I had a blast being terrified, and really enjoyed end game with the Prawn. Looking forward to playing Below Zero. Thank you Devs

I bought Subnautica a while ago and this game's genuinely grew on me. Subnautica 2 just came out a day ago as i'm posting this comment. I loved Subnautica a lot as a child till now and Subnautica 2 looks amazing but Subnautica 1 has some stuttering issues and I still can't find the issue, could someone help me? Good game though I enjoyed every second of it

Subnautica is the best of what survival games have to offer providing balanced difficulty (an illusion we'll get to later), freedom of exploration, and a near-perfect narrative direction. The main flaws I saw with this game was that I couldn't play it longer. Spoilers hidden.

The world of Subnautica is beautiful. The biodiversity in both biomes and their local flora and fauna is incredible. Having read most if not all of the databank entries, I can also say they did a nice job of providing in-universe lore for all the objects you scan. The Kelp Forests, Grand Reefs, and Lost River stood out to me the most, and so far, nothing I've seen in Subnautica 2 has compared to those. [spoiler]The world design also includes neat loop-backs and extra entrances to the Lost River.[/spoiler] I'm sure everyone's first run of this game can differ quite a bit, despite it being a linear story and progression.  You also have a constant waypoint in the game, and no I don't mean beacons, but the giant Aurora shipwreck. You can see it from anywhere on the map, and before you get the compass, it can act as a great directional tool. It also adds a nice, but devastating view, when you're on the surface.

The pacing of the game wonderfully simulates how a "lost at sea" survival situation might be. The beginning is completely chaotic. You frequently hear radio signals from other downed lifepods, you're worried about storage space in your own, and food and water are a constant concern. Eventually, you set up a base to rest your head. You start hearing less and less signals from other potential survivors. [spoiler]You're starting to get your stuff together, and then suddenly it seems a ship may be coming to rescue you. Or... not. The whole story opens up here. You're officially alone, and there's many mysteries to uncover.[/spoiler] Now, you have much more freedom to explore this vast ocean world and choose your own direction to go, with the occasional lifepod or Degasi PDA to point you in the right direction. I think its the perfect amount of hand-holding, not enough to make it boring or too linear, but enough to stop you from getting totally stuck and confused. 

The difficulty. One of the most important aspects of a survival game in my opinion, and one of the widest ranges of options in that genre (at least in terms of PvE). Project Zomboid is so hard that you dying is the point of the game. Valheim can be punishing but allows you to grind for enough gear to make it reasonable. Subnautica, truthfully, is not very difficult despite being mostly unable to kill the largest monsters in the game. It uses the illusion of fear as a way to pretend its difficult. The first time I heard a reaper leviathan, I did everything in my power to not even see it, carefully following along the side of the Aurora to find the location to board. [spoiler]When I found an entrance to the Lost River guarded by a baby Ghost Leviathan, I cautiously piloted my Prawn around it, and when finally spotted, freaked out and swung along the cavern walls like I was an underwater Spiderman.[/spoiler]However, once I took damage from a leviathan for the first time, in any of the vehicles, I realized they weren't really a threat. You could even avoid them pretty easily, and once you were exposed to them enough times, the fear left, and with it, any sense of the game's enemies being challenging. Of course, some of the other PvE elements introduce difficulty, but for the most part are not very challenging. Oxygen is more of a limitation than a challenge. You acquire better tanks and the Seamoth early, allowing you to get advanced materials and scan deeper fragments easily. Food and water are the usual survival mechanic, but food in this game is almost never a problem and the speed of water consumption is frankly, annoying. It does encourage you to set up sustainable solutions in your base, which is one of my greatest draws to the genre in general, but it doesn't feel challenging in a meaningful way. Also, the water consumption and acquisition process is so far so much worse in Subnautica 2, especially playing with friends.

This definitely belongs in the pantheon of survival games. However, I'd be remiss not mention my biggest problem with the game: the pacing at the end of the game. Fortunately, I'm a huge planner and base builder, so once I reached Lost River, I spent a lot of time setting up a forward outpost and exploring. However, I feel the game is basically over once you get there. I probably spent about 10-15 hours building the extra base, exploring, decking out my Cyclops, and bringing home tons of resources to expand my main base. Without that, I think you could go from Lost River to the end of the game in 5-10 hours. [Obviously, the game is meant to speed up as you acquire better machines, but it feels [spoiler] like the Lost River is really just an entrance for the Lava Lakes, which are really small themselves. I didn't even find the Sea Emperor to be all that threatening, and for the most point I really never even saw him. The alien structures within both Lava Lakes are amazing though and I love scanning things and reading the lore. [/spoiler]

[spoiler]The single worst part of the end's pacing is that the last thing you do in the story is a fetch quest... in a game that pretty much allows you to explore however you want. You have to get one of each type of seed to craft the hatching enzymes, and sure, its convenient as all of the teleporters are right there, but its incredibly boring and just destroys the gameplay for me. My main question and concern with this kind of quest is: if the game is going to give you everything you need, what is the point of making me run around grabbing and crafting it? It also ruins the potential for the teleporters as well, which could have been activated throughout the game to allow you to make several forward bases. By this point, I felt myself tired and trying to speedrun the end of the game rather than appreciating its approaching finale. I also found the "we flow through time together" story beat a little at odds with the gameplay, but the story overall was charming and well written.[/spoiler]

This game grabbed me like almost no other has. Every choice I made felt completely in my hands, though I was often drove by fear. I planned excursions, built a base that I would feel happy to actually live in, and explored different biomes even when I didn't need to. I was completely immersed and often felt that maybe I was really a submarine captain. I definitely could have put another 10-20 hours into this game expanding my underwater network and collecting every egg, plant, etc. if not for some of the pacing for the end.
Final Rating: 9/10

[h1]Reignated my passion for games[/h1]
[hr][/hr][i]Subnautica felt like gaming back then. Then you first start doing something which you love. World feels like your oyster. You don't know what lies behind next corner. World feels big and dangerous. Even as gameplay experience shifted through my invested hours, it still felt that it's one of those games which you couldn't play enough back in the day.[/i][hr][/hr]
[h1]Phases of Experience[/h1]
Game feels distinctively different the more you play. At first, you are hit with world full of wonder. Then game transforms into tasks and structured exploration. Then into completing objectives and grinding. Each stage with a game was different.
[list]
[b][i]First 10 Hours[/i][/b]
[*]This is what vows people. Game feels like real world. It's beautiful. Everywhere you go, you discover something. You feel intimidated by early enemies. See leviathians in a distance. Get jump scared by those exploding bastards in a cave. Game is wonderful as it eases you into itself, starting from a safe harbour. Getting then into enemies which scare you more than are dangerous. Then asks you to go to kelp forest which is dark and full of angry sharks. You are discovering ton of wreckage. Figuring out how to survive. Unlocking item after item. Aurora explodes in a distance and you are afraid of its radiation. You go into mundane cave as it would be your grand adventure, gathering ordinary crafting materials as they would be rare.
https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3740424518
Game is amazing in this period. Everywhere you go is a world full of posibilities and challenges. Your first pods. Your first fear of going through kelp forest. Your first deeper dives into 100 meters of water. Encountering of ground beater sharks. Descending down to mushroom cave system for those building schematics. Discovering full island and its history. Eventually culminating at receiveing call from incoming ship only to encounter massive alien facility. Swimming there at night through deep waters, concerned but determined. Game is really cooking there and is amongst the best games in existence.
 
[b][i]Between 20 to 30 Hours[/i][/b]
This is where charm and dazzle starts to wear off. At first it starts from game giving you more advanced blueprints. These blueprints at this point aren't very grindy, but it causes you to become goal oriented. You cease your amazement fueled exploration and become goal oriented. You start thinking in which zones you need to go to get these materials for these things you want to build. You start thinking about where your visited zones are on a map. You might not know it yet, but you are building a mental map of most of the game. World ceases to be huge and becomes compact, entirety of it held within your memory. Random places on the map cease to become of any interest to you. You become driven by the end goal. You want to gather materials for that new vehicle or building. You want to go to Aurora and turn off radiation and explore its interior. At this point there is still some exploration left as you are likely to start going deep underwater. Stumbling on Lost River - end game zone. It's amazing the first time you go, but it quickly become a tedious resource grind as you need to come multiple times there for rare resources, for alien buildings which are out of the way and a little bit hidden.

Personally, I still loved this part. It was a period when game ceased to be viewed with childlike joy and I became more of gamer. Feeling confident within its systems. Mastering game world and discovering everything it has to offer. Whatever it's good or bad.

[b][i]After 30 Hours[/i][/b]
Around this point, game becomes rather annoying for a lot of people. Game requires you to gather a lot of resources for Neptune, Cyclops or late game modules or base expansion. You will need to do supply runs solely for materials and often several, bringing specific materials to stock up on. This is where game collapses for many as it can become grindy. It's nowhere near other survival games as progress is still straightforward, but with just a little bit of grind. 

It's also a moment when you start noticing how limited game is. How toothless levathians are. How Emperor class levathians often just clip into terrain. How trivial it's to avoid all predators. The only place left is to do the final quest which can be quite a grind if you don't put 2 and 2 together and figure out unsaid solution that alien teleporters lead you to place where needed materials are nearby. Even then, it requires you to put 3 and 3 together and place a beacon next to teleport gate in order not to go all the way back. Game becomes very gamey at this point. You also start noticing how Lost River zones are rather custom made, gamey. They do not feel natural at all and are artificially created just to stun player when he goes through there for a first time. At N time, it becomes least impressive part of the terrain game has to offer.
https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3740427508
Personally, I didn't minded the grind. I loved game enough to finish it. For me it ended right when it started to overstay its welcome. For me, game was amazing at the start and great at middle and end. Though, it's not type of game which you would like to play more than once as once you learned it, it immediately loses that special quality which made it special. 
[/list]

[h1]The Bad[/h1]
Well, game is showing its age. Despite being very demanding on modern systems, game engine has very limited draw distance.
https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3740423414
There are very limited amount of game objects too. At first it might appear a lot, but then you start noticing that ground is often just a single texture, object. Highly optimized and with nothing on it. It has few points of interest with items and nothing around it. Game needs a graphical upgrade. Subnautica 2 is exactly that and graphical fidelity for such game goes a long way. Especially lighting solutions underwater, raytracing technologies could pay huge dividends. 

Another issue with a game is that it's rather safe. Nothing in this game is that threatening. World becomes hollow when you realize that you don't need to be careful and nothing can surprise you. This is when it falls apart. The fact that world is static and zones will always be the same with same unthreatening enemies takes away massively from replayability. At the end, I started noticing that predators are spawned each time PRECISELY at the same place with same movement. Game lacks any dynamism and credible threat to make world worth being in. Even then, game completely undermines itself by relegating all resources into specific, high density places, making any wide exploration of zones and random caves completely and utterly pointless.

The only real downside is that I was tricked to play as a minority. I was shocked to discover that when seeing my own shadow. Though, it's a game and we have to suspend our disbelief. Otherwise, our character would just had sunk as he just couldn't swim and that would be it. Realistic, but not very exciting gameplay.

[h1]Simply a Masterpiece[/h1]
[hr][/hr][i]It's rare when you play something which is so good that you can recommend to any gamer. This is such game. It's amazing experience which stands amongst greatest games ever created. If developers are not going to mess up sequel, we will have even better version of it. Even if this game doesn't have the replay value to stand amognst my Classic games, it still deserves to be at least tried by everyone. It's what gaming is all about.[/i][hr][/hr]

[url=https://steamcommunity.com/groups/Calicifer/discussions/0/562533105473229826/][i]Calicifer's Reviews[/i][/url]
[quote]If you are interested reading full review or want to discuss something with me, please follow the provided link.

i have thalassophobia, tbh..
but one of my beloved viewer asked me to try this game and i'm glad i did. i had to squint my eyes couple of times cuz of fear and i had to run back (almost the whole game) to my safe pod. i learned slowly, yes.. but i'm so proud i did my very best to progress the story!
✓ i managed to upgrade my life-saving equipments (O2 tank, fins, knife, etc etc)
✓ i managed to fix the radio and my safe pod!
✓ i managed to find materials that i need to make stuffs (LOL)
it maybe nothing to some people, but it's a big achievement for me!!

streamed it for 3 hours without i even noticed! couldn't get off the chair and my body felt stiff just like being beaten by a whole bunch of classmates, but i'm planning to go back and study more about this game. it's THAT good, it makes me wanna play again and again despite of the fear.

RECOMMENDED!!
this is scary because markiplier is in the game and this means the bite of 87 freddy fast bear is real
an amazing survival game with a beautiful underwater world and fun exploration. It reminds me of Minecraft because of its crafting and survival elements, but it feels more immersive and atmospheric.
The progression loop and story surrounding the missing lifepods makes for one of the more addicting exploration game I have played before

fun game with a great story. things got weird near the end but it's chill

i hope someone finds my time capsule one day

I had heard of the game only by name before and decided to finally buy it when it was on sale. I had never been equal amounts terrified and amazed while playing a game. That said, I am stoked to finish this game and then play Below Zero and Subnautica 2!

I'd been wanting to play this for a very long time, basically since it was in its early access days and I watched Jacksepticeye play it, but finally got the chance to now as the second game is coming out. It's such a wonderful and beautiful experience with a good amount of challenge mixed in, but it's definitely not too hard for new or experienced gamers once you figure out what to do.

I hate my 11 year old self "finishing" this game in creative mode on his PS4, spoiling his nearly 19 year old self all those years later. I wish I had experienced this game for the first time when I bought this on PC.

Subnautica is a pretty good game, even if it's dated due to it's age. Finished it in 34.6 hours. I'm hoping Subnautica 2 has more polish and is generally an even better game.

Jumped into it with the hype for Subnautica 2 and definitely recommend anyone to jump in better late than never! Great game with lots to get lost into and for the price it goes on sale for, it's a no brainer.

10/10
Played this game for at least 100+ hours on my Xbox and got it with the Subnautica 2 bundle and decided to play it again and it did not fall short from last time I had played, The game is just as fun as I remember and all aspects are great. Excited to see how well Subnautica 2 holds up against this gem.
One of the best underwater survival games! This game is very awesome and fun.
this game is so peak and fun. some might quit because they think that the ocean is too scary, but the longer you play it oooh it gets better and better.

I did NOT expect this much from Subnautica.

The concept of being stranded in an alien planet is so fascinating and that's what got me hooked at first, but I was not ready for what was waiting for me.
The game is full of surprises and unexpected twists, new discoveries that change the way you play and keep the game feeling like a new experience over and over.
I love Subnautica's scientific etiquette, going around and [spoiler]scanning everything[/spoiler], [spoiler]reading about new data you find[/spoiler], the game is just great at keeping you hooked in.

I consider Subnautica to be a must play for anyone desires to build videogame culture, this to me is one of the best survival games ever and I'm going to die on this hill.
Outstanding game, one of the best of the 2010s. Tons to explore and discover - don't spoil yourself. Not as scary as it seems, BTW.

Honestly if you've never seen anything about this game at all after so many years, don't read the reviews, its already got an overwhelmingly positive, just buy it, on sale or not this game is an experience like no other. Don't spoil too much for yourself and have fun on a blind playthrough, the way god intended.
Best underwater survival game with horror elements

DUDE. I've been so locked tf in to this game for like a week straight and I am having the best time!!! I'm not sure if it's supposed to be a horror game - maybe to people afraid of ocean exploration - but seriously this game is rad as hell, you can do, see, explore, and built so many different cool things and depending on which things you build you could have a completely different experience than I am. I can't wait to keep playing and see what else this game has in store
very very fun, it has quickly become my favorite game and the ending is bitter sweet

An incredible game that is a must play in my opinion. The story is fantastic but gives you plenty of room to play your way and at your own pace. The crafting and building systems are great. What's really the best part, though, is the world. Its so incredibly detailed and unique that you can always find something new to explore. It infuses you with such a sense of wonder and awe that it's hard to put down. Fantastic game and easy 10/10.

I honestly don't even know where to start y'all. 

I remember when Subnautica was an early access title with only a handful of YouTubers being able to get there hands on it. I used to watch Dev Logs and Letsplays before I owned a computer. Coming back to the game years after it's release, I can confidently say that this game is one of my top 10 games OAT. From the soundtrack to the PDA guiding you through your tenure on 4546B, this game nails the feeling of surviving on a planet in solitude after all of your crewmates were either eaten alive or died in a number of tragic ways. One of my favorite quotes comes from this game, that being the infamous, "Detecting multiple LEVIATHAN class lifeforms in the region. Are you certain whatever you're doing is worth it." Hearing that for the first time after picking up the game again, it sent straight chills down my spine. And I mean, c'mon, you can't talk about this game without talking about the Reaper Leviathan. I clipped my first interaction with it and you can literally see the fear in my eyes after hearing his roar and turning around in my Seamoth just to be face to face with that mans chompers. Beautiful base building mechanics and wonderful story that makes me want to replay the game and continue my save even though I just finished it. Of course, I had to add this title to my 100% catalogue as it deserved a spot among the GOATS. 

Unfortunately Subnautica : Below Zero hasn't been able to give me the same feeling as the first but sequels rarely live up to the hype of their predecessors. Except Terminator 2 : Judgement Day. I can't be too harsh though as I've only put about 2-3 hours into the game so far and haven't even started building my base, which was my favorite part about the original and the newly released title in the series. Anyways, WONDERFUL game that I was very pleased to play through again. I never give out 10/10 for anything, especially video games, as most either fall into the 9/10 or 6/10 range for me but this game absolutely deserves it for the sole fact that Riley Robinson is a badass.

Thank you Unknown Worlds for this gem, I hope Subnautica 2 can bring me the same joy and satisfaction as this awesome masterpiece :)

I have never in my life lived through an experience like this.

The game started with a few simple sandy dunes in shallow water and opened up into a vast world full of odd and often dangerous creatures. I would never have dreamt of feeling such pressure towards the end, due to one specific mob camping one of my vehicles. This certainly has been an unforgettable adventure with a really well written plot, lots of details that help the player understand their new environment and one too many heart attacks deep below the surface. A true masterpiece in my opinion, despite I'm deadly afraid of certain creatures in this game.

It is definitely worth getting. 10/10, would scream again.

I first learned about this game before I even entered middle school. I followed its development and always wanted to play it, though for some reason I never did. After Subnautica 2 came out in Early Access, I decided now was a good time to finally play this game. It was incredible. The world, the creatures, the ecosystems, everything is fantastic. The creatures seem familiar while still seeming alien, and the ecosystems make sense. The little details really make them feel fleshed out as organisms, and makes the ecology more complete. It has the perfect amount of comedy sprinkled in and it balances being terrifying and fostering exploration well.

Don't be like me and wait years to try this game. It's worth getting now

I've played this early on Xbox when I was a child in 2020, experimenting with the creative mode and trying to find what the story was like. Now, I am reliving the joys of my childhood on survival mode on my PC. I hope for the best for the dev studio behind Subnautica and its sequel, because holy crap, this is genuinely worth the price. Regardless if it's on sale for 10$ or not for 40$. I've still got the original game disc for it.

I'm genuinely amazed by Subnautica! I was immersed, engaged, and addicted to its gameplay loop :3
The pacing became quite stiff in the later part of the game, when I was just mainly grinding resources, but that didn't stop me from enjoying my time, building a cool base, and gearing up for the final journey into the depths of the ocean.

Probably in my top 10 of all time. Maybe not a ton of hours you can spend in this game in terms of replayablility, but those 25-50 hours you will get for the playthrough is the most intense type of fun you will experience.

absolutely amazing game. it accomplishes what most of modern day horror games can't: a true sense of fear. it doesnt need to rely on cheap jumpscares, the ambience and atmosphere of the game is so phenomenal that i cannot stop coming back to this game every now and then. cant wait to buy subnautica 2.

If youre coming from Subnautica 2, yes, you can still play this game. It doesnt have the quality of life stuff Subnautica 2 has BUT you can literally install mods that give you all you need. The modding community has kept this game alive for many many years but even without mods you can totally enjoy it and its story.

This is a very fun exploration and thriller game, I would recommend
its an very good survival game and its super fun.
if you have the mildest sense of fear of the ocean or deep water, this is a horror game 10 times scarier then the scariest game youve ever played, if you dont, its a chill sandbox game.

Genuienly one of the best games of last generation, it has THE best opening hours, early and mid game in the genre and it has spectacular visuals even to this day.

The sound design and the atmosphere in general is top class and very immersive, it almost sounds like arc raiders with it's loud and overwhelming sounds, truly an amazing experience in that regard.
Another amazing aspect of the game is the simplicity and fun resource gathering (pre end game) which take the montotony out where most games in the genre suffer.

The biggest miss in this game is easily the end game, without saying too much the game starts feeling like a giant run around for resources that you would have never looked at unless you knew what was required in the end game, this leads to hours of navigating back and fourth through the same 2-4 bioms which are far af and don't feel fun to navigate.

All in all the experience is amazing and very refreshing, even if you don't like these survival games this one stands above all.
I like the open world experience and the creative liberties that the game offered to me on what pace i wanted to progress the game at.

This is top 5 or 10 games of all time. Underwater minecraft with great exploration and good puzzles with good base building. If your excuse of not getting this game is because its a 'horror game', then im sorry to say but iron man dies at the end of avengers endgame.

I’m here after playing Subnautica 2 for about 20 hours in early access, and since I am apparently completely hooked on this universe, I thought it’d be a good idea to go back and start where it all begins. Saw the original on sale for 75% off and figured there’s literally never been a better time to dive in.

Going backwards like this is actually pretty interesting because it gives you a massive appreciation for the dynamic improvements they’re working on for the sequel. For example, in the first game, you manually have to have the minerals or supplies right on your person to use the fabricator, rather than the system just pulling automatically from nearby base storage. It’s nothing crazy or game-ruining, just one of those quality-of-life differences you notice immediately.

Speaking of animations, playing the original makes you realize how much detail went into the first game compared to some of the rough edges in Subnautica 2’s alpha. Take the air bladder—in this first game, the animation is great and the character holds it properly. In the sequel, you notice the button is actually on the bottom and you're holding the whole thing upside down. It’s obviously just one of those funny early access oversights that I'm sure they'll fix, but going backward really lets you spot those little details.

That said, the original game holds up incredibly well. It’s great to see that the quality of the graphics was just as nice back then as they are now; the game is absolutely gorgeous. The atmosphere is unmatched—the transition from the bright, beautiful shallow reefs to the pitch-black, terrifying deep ocean is brilliant. What people absolutely love about this game (and what hooked me instantly) is how it balances that pure awe of exploration with genuine terror, all without relying on cheap jump scares. The sound design alone, from the ambient music to the distant roar of a Reaper Leviathan, is a masterpiece.

It’s not perfect, of course. Being an older indie title, it has some legendary quirks. The pop-in can be pretty bad when you’re moving fast in a vehicle—you’ll literally watch terrain and wreckage render right in front of your face. There are also the classic physics glitches, like your Seamoth occasionally clipping into a island or getting stuck inside a solid rock wall if you park it too close to a texture.

But honestly? None of that takes away from the experience. For a game that came out years ago, it’s a masterclass in survival design. If you loved what you saw in the early looks of the sequel, getting this on sale is a no-brainer. It’s a beautiful, terrifying ride.

4 hours into this incredible and legendary game , i have no clue about the direction , yet i wanna keep playing and exploring. it feels satisfying when u finally do something. thalasaphobia kicks in so many times aswell. i would completely reccomend anyone to buy this , on sale its totally worth it.

Did not expect to like this game as survival crafting games are usually not my bag... and while I still had lots of complaints about the grindy nature of the game and genre itself, I immensely enjoyed this! 
I've never played a game that game me the same feelings of terror like being so small in a huge open world and running into various beautiful, yet unsettling creature. Some nice some, not so nice... 
Every "biome" in this game is unique, and I never stopped wanting to explore further and deeper. The silent protagonist and environmental story telling are just the cherry on top. Have to mention it's a little buggy though, and some of the resource gather got old especially towards the end...
Not overly difficult but no too easy either - truly rewarding for exploration, while not having a level of ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ that makes other challenging games feel like chores.

It's a great little survival game. Swimming around feels natural, the graphics are nice, the music is a banger. The gameplay loop is fairly standard, collect resources, refine them, make bigger stuff, advance the plot. It is pretty obvious how to progress, even if the lack of a compass is a big issue for people like me. My only complaint was the hitbox of the fish, it was really janky trying to fight them with a knife.

The only reason I refunded it is because I realised I no longer enjoy survival games - but this one is still a good one.

An absolute masterpiece in the exploration department, 100% would recommend.


Also caused my fear of the ocean.
One of the best survival base building underwater games ever made truly a classic. If you haven't played yet and you love survival or base building or sci fi then you should try it.

Subnautica is one of those games that is incredibly easy to get into. From the moment you enter this underwater world, it immediately creates an atmosphere that makes you want to explore.

It’s not just about survival — there is building as well — but everything feels completely different compared to other games because it all takes place deep under the sea. That gives it a truly unique vibe.

Exploration is the main focus, as there is always something new to discover: new places, items, and mysteries that push you to go further and further. The story is there too, but it’s not forced — it slowly unfolds in the background.

It’s definitely one of those games where you think “just a little bit more”… and suddenly it’s already night 😄

I highly recommend this game to anyone who enjoys survival and exploration games.

Rating: 11/10

It's personally one of my favourite games of all time. And with Subnautica 2 releasing later this day I'm just blown away. I highly reccomend this game if you like survival games and underwater creatures. You ARE going to poop your pants while playing this game.

Beautiful and not demanding graphics
Amazing story ( played 5 times )
Magnificent world to explore
Legit one of the best games i've played (hyped as hell for Subnautica 2)

Subnautica is one of my favorite survival games because it feels very unique and mysterious. The game is not extremely scary, but it still makes you nervous sometimes, especially when you go deeper into the ocean. I like how the fear comes from the unknown instead of cheap jump scares.

The creature sounds are amazing and probably the best part of the game. Sometimes you hear strange noises in the dark water before you see anything, and that makes the game feel realistic and tense. The Leviathans are scary, but also very interesting and memorable.

I also love the exploration. Every area looks different and has its own atmosphere. Finding caves, alien bases, and secrets made me want to continue playing all the time. The underwater world feels alive and beautiful.

The story is simple but very emotional. Even though you are alone most of the time, the game still makes you care about surviving and escaping the planet.

Overall, Subnautica is an incredible game with great atmosphere, mysterious sounds, and fun exploration. I would definitely recommend it to people who enjoy survival and adventure games.
Already have around 70 hours on PS4 but decided to buy on steam as well. This game is survival perfected and I'm praying that the second game lives up to its predecessor.
Fun game, doesnt take too long to play. Just take your time and enjoy!

EDIT: Great survival game. Has some major flaws but it recompensates with the gameplay, exploration, world design, etc. A must-try for some casual gamers. Last time I spend so much time on a game in such short period of time was ds3. 9/10

Subnautica is not just a survival game. It is a slow realization that the ocean does not care about you whatsoever and somehow that is exactly what makes it beautiful.

You start as a survivor and slowly become a cautious observer intruding into a world far older and calmer than yourself. Every biome, creature and sound feels memorable and intentional. Nothing feels like filler.

The gameplay loop is literally:
curiosity → fear → preparation → deeper descent → existential dread.

The lore is handled perfectly too. No constant yapping or forced exposition, just silence, discovery and isolation. The game trusts the player to think for themselves.

Atmospheric, melancholic and weirdly philosophical without trying too hard.

9.8/10
(0.2 deducted because eventually the game ends and nothing is inherently perfect. The ocean mentally does not.)

I cannot unsee what this game made my eyes process into my mind
Good game, definitely try it

I cant believe I missed this gem!

I never realized there was a story in this survival game, it gives you a sense of purpose that many survival games dont have.

Subnautica is one of those games I've heard and praised for years, yet it was also the game I kept putting off because of my fear of deep water. I can jump into almost any horror game without hesitation, but Subnautica always felt like a challenge I wasn't ready for.

With the sequel on the horizon, I finally decided to face that fear and give it a try. Playing Subnautica with thalassophobia can be genuinely terrifying at times, but that's also what makes the experience so memorable. Beyond the fear, the game gave me a newfound appreciation for the ocean even more and its mysteries. What started as something I avoided ended up becoming one of my favorite gaming experiences and easily one of the best survival games I've ever played.

[spoiler]Although I'm appalled with the supposed debt the player's facing at the end. 1 TRILLION CREDITS?! The player did not experience and survive all that just to be hindered by huge debt in the end T_T [/spoiler]
